expanded_description: Drawing organic molecules accurately is a foundational skill in organic chemistry. This section provides comprehensive practice in converting between structural formulas, condensed formulas, and line-angle (skeletal) diagrams. Mastery of these representations enables clear communication of molecular structure, prediction of reactivity, and interpretation of spectroscopic data.

core_concept.principle: Multiple Representations Serve Different Purposes in Organic Chemistry
core_concept.explanation: Organic molecules can be depicted using structural formulas (showing all atoms and bonds), condensed formulas (showing connectivity in linear text), and line-angle diagrams (showing carbon skeleton with implied hydrogens). Each representation has advantages: structural formulas clarify bonding for beginners, condensed formulas save space for simple molecules, and line-angle diagrams efficiently depict complex structures. Proficiency requires bidirectional conversion between all three formats.

image.alt: Diagram showing three representations of butane: structural, condensed, and line-angle
image.caption: Three representations of butane: expanded structural (all atoms shown), condensed ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$), and line-angle (zigzag line with 4 vertices).

sections.introduction: Structural formulas, also called expanded or Lewis structures, show every atom and every bond in a molecule explicitly. This representation is pedagogically valuable for understanding bonding patterns, hybridization, and electron distribution, though it becomes cumbersome for large molecules.

key_concept.definition: A structural formula depicts all atoms as elemental symbols and all covalent bonds as lines (single, double, or triple), with lone pairs optionally shown. Every carbon must have four bonds, every nitrogen three bonds plus a lone pair, every oxygen two bonds plus two lone pairs, and every hydrogen one bond.
key_concept.context: Structural formulas are essential for learning VSEPR geometry, identifying functional groups, and understanding reaction mechanisms. They are rarely used for molecules larger than $\text{C}_6$ due to visual clutter.

image.alt: Expanded structural formula of ethanol showing all atoms and bonds
image.caption: Ethanol structural formula: all $\text{C}$, $\text{H}$, $\text{O}$ atoms and bonds shown explicitly; lone pairs on oxygen optional.

principles.statement: Carbon atoms form exactly four bonds in neutral organic molecules; hydrogen forms one; oxygen forms two; nitrogen forms three; halogens form one.
principles.application: When drawing structural formulas, verify that each atom has the correct number of bonds. Missing bonds indicate missing hydrogens or incorrect connectivity.
principles.analogy: Think of atoms as having a fixed number of 'hands' for holding bonds: carbon has 4 hands, hydrogen has 1, oxygen has 2. Every hand must be holding something (another atom or a lone pair).
principles.example: In ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$): the $\text{CH}_3$ carbon has three $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds and one $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond (4 total); the $\text{CH}_2$ carbon has two $\text{C}-\text{H}$, one $\text{C}-\text{C}$, one $\text{C}-\text{O}$ (4 total); oxygen has one $\text{C}-\text{O}$ and one $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond (2 total, plus two lone pairs).

principles.statement: Structural formulas should reflect approximate molecular geometry: tetrahedral carbons drawn with $\approx 109.5^\circ$ angles, trigonal planar with $\approx 120^\circ$, linear with $180^\circ$.
principles.application: Drawing bonds at appropriate angles helps visualize steric effects, predict reactivity, and understand conformational preferences. Avoid drawing tetrahedral carbons as perfect squares or crosses.
principles.analogy: Drawing molecular geometry is like sketching a 3D object on paper: use angles and perspective to suggest the true shape, even though the drawing is 2D.
principles.example: In propene, the double-bonded carbons are drawn with $120^\circ$ bond angles (trigonal planar), while the methyl carbon is drawn with tetrahedral angles ($\approx 109.5^\circ$).

worked_examples.title: Drawing the Structural Formula of 2-Methylpropane
worked_examples.problem: Draw the complete structural formula for 2-methylpropane (isobutane), showing all atoms and bonds.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the carbon skeleton: 2-methylpropane has a 3-carbon parent chain (propane) with a methyl branch at carbon 2.
Draw the central carbon with four bonds: one to each end carbon, one to the methyl branch, one to a hydrogen.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Central C: bonded to C, C, C, H}$

step_by_step.explanation: Add hydrogens to terminal carbons to satisfy tetravalency: each end carbon needs three hydrogens.
Verify atom count: 4 carbons, 10 hydrogens = $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ (correct for alkane).

worked_examples.final_answer: Structural formula: central carbon bonded to three $\text{CH}_3$ groups and one H. All bonds shown as lines; all atoms labeled. Molecular formula: $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Structural formulas require explicit depiction of all atoms and bonds; verify tetravalency for each carbon.

image.caption: 2-Methylpropane structural formula: central carbon with three methyl groups and one hydrogen.

sections.introduction: Condensed structural formulas represent molecules in a linear text format by grouping atoms bonded to each carbon. This format saves space while preserving connectivity information, making it ideal for writing reactions, naming compounds, and communicating simple structures in text.

categories.description: Atoms bonded to each carbon are written consecutively without bond lines
categories.detailed_mechanism: Write carbon atoms in sequence, with attached hydrogens written immediately after each carbon. Substituents in parentheses indicate branching. Multiple identical groups use subscripts: $\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_4\text{CH}_3$ for hexane.

examples.explanation: Expanded: $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$. Condensed: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$ or $\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_2\text{CH}_3$. Parentheses group repeating $\text{CH}_2$ units.
Condensed: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)\text{CH}_3$ or $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CH}$. Parentheses indicate the methyl branch attached to the second carbon.
examples.equation: $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CH} \text{ = three methyl groups on one carbon}$

categories.description: Heteroatoms and multiple bonds are shown explicitly within the condensed sequence
categories.detailed_mechanism: Write functional groups as they appear in the carbon chain: $\text{OH}$ for alcohols, $\text{CHO}$ for aldehydes, $\text{COOH}$ for carboxylic acids. Double/triple bonds shown with $=$ or $\equiv$: $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2$ for ethene.

examples.explanation: Condensed: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$ or $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$. The $\text{OH}$ group is written at the end to indicate attachment to the terminal carbon.
Condensed: $\text{CH}_2=\text{CHCHO}$. The $\text{CHO}$ indicates an aldehyde group; the $=$ indicates the double bond between carbons 1 and 2.
Condensed: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOCH}_2\text{CH}_3$ or $\text{CH}_3\text{C}(=\text{O})\text{OCH}_2\text{CH}_3$. The $\text{COO}$ sequence indicates an ester linkage.

categories.description: Parentheses group substituents attached to a specific carbon in the main chain
categories.detailed_mechanism: When a carbon has branches, write the main chain carbons in order and place branches in parentheses immediately after the carbon to which they attach. Multiple identical branches use subscripts outside the parentheses.

examples.explanation: Condensed: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)\text{CH}(\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3)\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$. Methyl at C2, ethyl at C3, both in parentheses after their attachment carbons.

image.alt: Condensed formula showing branching with parentheses
image.caption: Parentheses indicate branches: $\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)$ = carbon with methyl substituent.

examples.explanation: Condensed: $\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_4$ or $(\text{CH}_3)_4\text{C}$. Central carbon bonded to four methyl groups; subscript 4 indicates four identical substituents.

sections.key_concept: Condensed formulas: write carbons in sequence, attach hydrogens directly, use parentheses for branches, subscripts for repeats. Functional groups ($\text{OH}$, $\text{CHO}$, $\text{COOH}$) shown explicitly.

sections.introduction: Line-angle diagrams, also called skeletal structures or bond-line formulas, are the most efficient representation for organic molecules. Carbon atoms are implied at line vertices and endpoints; hydrogens attached to carbons are omitted (assumed to complete tetravalency); heteroatoms and their attached hydrogens are shown explicitly.

principles.statement: In line-angle diagrams: (1) each vertex and line endpoint represents a carbon atom, (2) hydrogens bonded to carbons are not shown but are assumed to satisfy tetravalency, (3) heteroatoms (O, N, S, halogens) are shown explicitly with their attached hydrogens.
principles.application: To determine the number of hydrogens on a carbon in a skeletal structure: count the bonds shown to that carbon, then add hydrogens to reach four total bonds. For example, a carbon with two shown bonds has two implied hydrogens.
principles.analogy: Think of skeletal structures as a 'carbon skeleton' with the 'flesh' (hydrogens) implied. Just as you can infer a person's full shape from their skeleton, you can infer all hydrogens from the carbon framework.
principles.example: In a zigzag line with 5 segments: there are 6 carbon atoms (endpoints + vertices). Terminal carbons have 3 implied H's ($\text{CH}_3$); internal carbons have 2 implied H's ($\text{CH}_2$); a carbon with a branch has 1 implied H ($\text{CH}$); a carbon with two branches has 0 implied H's (quaternary C).

principles.statement: Double bonds are shown as two parallel lines; triple bonds as three parallel lines. Bond angles should reflect hybridization: $\approx 120^\circ$ for $sp^2$ carbons, $180^\circ$ for $sp$ carbons, tetrahedral angles implied for $sp^3$.
principles.application: Drawing appropriate angles helps convey molecular shape and stereochemistry. For alkenes, draw the double bond with $120^\circ$ angles to show trigonal planar geometry; for alkynes, draw the triple bond linearly.
principles.analogy: Skeletal diagrams are like architectural blueprints: the angles and line styles convey 3D information in a 2D drawing. Proper angles help the viewer 'see' the molecule's shape.
principles.example: In cyclohexene, the double bond is drawn with $120^\circ$ angles at the $sp^2$ carbons, while the rest of the ring uses tetrahedral-angle zigzags. The double bond is shown as two parallel lines.

principles.statement: Atoms other than carbon and hydrogen (O, N, S, P, halogens) are written as elemental symbols. Hydrogens attached to heteroatoms are shown explicitly because they are not implied by the skeletal convention.
principles.application: When drawing alcohols, amines, carboxylic acids, etc., always show the $\text{O}-\text{H}$, $\text{N}-\text{H}$, or $\text{S}-\text{H}$ bonds. Lone pairs are optional but often omitted for clarity.
principles.analogy: Heteroatoms are like 'special guests' at a party: they get name tags (elemental symbols) and their companions (attached hydrogens) are introduced explicitly, unlike the regular carbon/hydrogen crowd.
principles.example: Ethanol skeletal: zigzag line ending in $-\text{OH}$ (oxygen shown, hydrogen on oxygen shown). Ethylamine: zigzag ending in $-\text{NH}_2$ (nitrogen and both hydrogens shown).

worked_examples.title: Converting Structural to Line-Angle: 3-Methylpentane
worked_examples.problem: Convert the structural formula of 3-methylpentane to a line-angle diagram.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the carbon skeleton: 5-carbon parent chain with a methyl branch at carbon 3.
Draw the parent chain as a zigzag line with 5 vertices (6 carbons total? No: 5 segments = 6 endpoints/vertices, but pentane has 5 carbons, so 4 segments = 5 vertices).
Add the methyl branch at the third carbon vertex as a short line extending from that vertex.
Verify: count vertices = 5 carbons in chain + 1 carbon in branch = 6 total carbons. Molecular formula: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}$.

worked_examples.final_answer: Line-angle diagram: zigzag line with 4 segments (5 carbons), with a short branch line at the third vertex. All hydrogens implied; no labels needed.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Skeletal diagrams: vertices = carbons, lines = bonds, hydrogens on carbons implied, branches shown as additional lines.

image.caption: 3-Methylpentane skeletal: 5-carbon zigzag with methyl branch at third vertex.

worked_examples.title: Interpreting a Skeletal Structure: Determine Molecular Formula
worked_examples.problem: Given the line-angle diagram of a molecule with a 6-carbon zigzag chain and an $-\text{OH}$ group at carbon 2, determine the molecular formula.

step_by_step.explanation: Count carbon atoms: vertices and endpoints in the zigzag = 6 carbons.

Count hydrogens on each carbon: terminal carbons (2) have 3 H each; internal carbons without branches (3) have 2 H each; carbon with $-\text{OH}$ (1) has 1 H (since it has bonds to two carbons and one oxygen).
Add hydrogens attached to heteroatoms: the $-\text{OH}$ group has one hydrogen shown explicitly.
Count heteroatoms: one oxygen in the $-\text{OH}$ group.

worked_examples.final_answer: Molecular formula: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}\text{O}$. The molecule is hexan-2-ol.
worked_examples.concept_applied: To find molecular formula from skeletal: count carbons (vertices), calculate hydrogens (4 minus shown bonds per carbon), add explicit heteroatom hydrogens.

image.caption: Hexan-2-ol skeletal: 6-carbon zigzag with $-\text{OH}$ at second vertex.

sections.introduction: Proficiency in organic chemistry requires fluency in converting between structural, condensed, and line-angle representations. This skill is essential for interpreting literature, solving problems, and communicating structures efficiently. Practice bidirectional conversion to build intuition.

categories.description: Collapse explicit bonds into linear text while preserving connectivity
categories.detailed_mechanism: Read the structural formula left to right (or along the main chain). For each carbon, write the elemental symbol followed by attached hydrogens as a subscript. Place branches in parentheses immediately after the carbon to which they attach. Use subscripts for repeating $\text{CH}_2$ groups.

examples.explanation: Structural: $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$ with all bonds drawn. Condensed: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$ or $\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_2\text{CH}_3$. Remove bond lines; group atoms by carbon.

image.alt: Conversion from structural to condensed formula
image.caption: Structural → condensed: remove bond lines, write atoms consecutively.

examples.explanation: 2-Methylbutane structural: central chain with methyl branch. Condensed: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$. Parentheses enclose the branch attached to the second carbon.

categories.description: Translate linear text into skeletal diagram by mapping carbons to vertices
categories.detailed_mechanism: Identify the main carbon chain in the condensed formula. Draw a zigzag line with one vertex per carbon. Add branches as short lines extending from the appropriate vertices. Show heteroatoms and their hydrogens explicitly. Omit all hydrogens attached to carbons.

examples.explanation: Condensed: $\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_3\text{CH}_3$. Line-angle: zigzag with 4 segments (5 vertices). No labels needed; all hydrogens implied.

image.alt: Conversion from condensed to line-angle formula
image.caption: Condensed → skeletal: each carbon becomes a vertex; hydrogens omitted.

examples.explanation: Condensed: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$. Line-angle: 3-segment zigzag ending in $-\text{OH}$ (oxygen and its hydrogen shown).

categories.description: Expand skeletal diagram to show all atoms and bonds explicitly
categories.detailed_mechanism: For each vertex/endpoint, write a carbon symbol. For each carbon, count shown bonds and add hydrogens to reach four total bonds. Draw all bonds as lines. Show heteroatoms and their attached hydrogens explicitly. Add lone pairs to heteroatoms if desired.

examples.explanation: Line-angle: hexagon. Structural: six $\text{CH}_2$ groups in a ring, each carbon bonded to two neighbors and two hydrogens. All 12 hydrogens shown explicitly.

image.alt: Conversion from line-angle to structural formula
image.caption: Skeletal → structural: add carbon labels and all implied hydrogens.

examples.explanation: Line-angle: zigzag with double bond and methyl branch. Structural: draw all carbons, add hydrogens to satisfy tetravalency, show double bond as two lines, place methyl branch explicitly.

worked_examples.title: Full Conversion Cycle: 2-Butanol
worked_examples.problem: Starting with the structural formula of 2-butanol, convert to condensed form, then to line-angle, then back to structural to verify.

step_by_step.explanation: Structural formula: $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{OH})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$ with all bonds drawn; oxygen has two lone pairs.
Convert to condensed: remove bond lines, group atoms by carbon, place $\text{OH}$ in parentheses after the carbon it attaches to.
Convert to line-angle: draw 4-carbon zigzag; at second vertex, add $-\text{OH}$ (show O and H explicitly); omit all C-bonded H's.
Convert back to structural: expand skeletal by adding carbon labels and implied hydrogens; verify connectivity matches original.

worked_examples.final_answer: All three representations describe the same molecule: 2-butanol ($\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}\text{O}$). Structural shows all details; condensed is compact text; skeletal is efficient for complex structures.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bidirectional conversion practice builds fluency: structural ↔ condensed ↔ skeletal. Verify by checking molecular formula and connectivity at each step.

image.alt: 2-Butanol shown in structural, condensed, and line-angle formats
image.caption: 2-Butanol in three representations: structural (detailed), condensed (compact), skeletal (efficient).

sections.introduction: Drawing organic molecules accurately requires attention to conventions and avoidance of common errors. This section summarizes best practices for each representation type and highlights frequent mistakes to avoid in homework, exams, and professional communication.

principles.statement: Choose the representation appropriate for the context: structural for learning bonding, condensed for text communication, skeletal for complex molecules and reaction mechanisms.
principles.application: In organic chemistry courses, skeletal diagrams are preferred for molecules larger than $\text{C}_4$ because they reduce clutter and emphasize functional groups and connectivity. Use structural formulas only when explicitly requested or when teaching fundamental concepts.
principles.analogy: Choosing a drawing style is like choosing a map scale: a street map (structural) is great for learning a neighborhood, but a highway map (skeletal) is better for planning a cross-country trip. Use the right tool for the job.
principles.example: Drawing a steroid molecule: structural formula would require hundreds of atoms and be unreadable; skeletal diagram clearly shows the four-ring framework and functional groups in a compact format.

principles.statement: Draw skeletal structures with clean, consistent angles: use zigzags for $sp^3$ chains, $120^\circ$ angles for $sp^2$ centers, and straight lines for $sp$ centers. Avoid ambiguous intersections or overlapping lines.
principles.application: Proper angles convey hybridization and geometry, which affect reactivity and stereochemistry. A poorly drawn alkene with unclear double bond geometry can lead to misinterpretation of cis/trans isomerism.
principles.analogy: Drawing clear skeletal structures is like writing legible handwriting: the viewer shouldn't have to guess what you meant. Clean lines and appropriate angles prevent miscommunication.
principles.example: In drawing (Z)-2-butene, show the double bond with $120^\circ$ angles and place the two methyl groups on the same side of the double bond to unambiguously indicate the Z configuration.

categories.description: Adding or omitting hydrogens incorrectly when converting between representations
categories.detailed_mechanism: Students often forget that skeletal diagrams imply hydrogens on carbons, leading to incorrect molecular formulas. Conversely, when expanding skeletal to structural, they may add too many or too few hydrogens by miscounting shown bonds.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: Counting only shown atoms in skeletal diagram and reporting $\text{C}_5\text{O}$ for pentanol. CORRECT: Add implied hydrogens: terminal carbons have 3 H, internal have 2 H, carbon with OH has 1 H → $\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12}\text{O}$.
INCORRECT: Drawing hydrogens on carbons in skeletal diagram. CORRECT: Omit all C-bonded H's; show only heteroatom H's (e.g., $-\text{OH}$, $-\text{NH}_2$).
examples.equation: $\text{Skeletal: H on C = implied; H on O/N = shown}$

categories.description: Drawing branches in a way that obscures which carbon they attach to
categories.detailed_mechanism: In skeletal diagrams, branches must originate clearly from a specific vertex. Drawing a branch line that appears to connect two vertices or float between carbons creates ambiguity about connectivity.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: Drawing a methyl branch as a line that starts midway along a bond. CORRECT: Draw the branch line starting precisely at the vertex (carbon) to which it attaches.

image.alt: Correct vs incorrect branch placement in skeletal diagrams
image.caption: Branches must originate at vertices, not along bonds.

categories.description: Drawing double/triple bonds with incorrect geometry or spacing
categories.detailed_mechanism: Double bonds should be drawn as two parallel lines with appropriate $120^\circ$ angles at $sp^2$ carbons. Triple bonds should be linear ($180^\circ$). Drawing them with tetrahedral angles or non-parallel lines misrepresents molecular geometry and can confuse stereochemistry.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: Drawing ethene as a square with a double bond. CORRECT: Draw with $120^\circ$ angles at each carbon to show trigonal planar geometry; double bond as two parallel lines.
INCORRECT: Drawing propyne with a bent triple bond. CORRECT: Draw the $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{C}$ unit as a straight line to reflect linear geometry at $sp$ carbons.

categories.description: Failing to show hydrogens attached to oxygen, nitrogen, or other heteroatoms in skeletal diagrams
categories.detailed_mechanism: The skeletal convention implies hydrogens only on carbons. Hydrogens attached to heteroatoms must be shown explicitly because they are chemically significant (e.g., acidic protons in $-\text{OH}$, basic protons in $-\text{NH}_2$).

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: Drawing ethanol as zigzag-$\text{O}$ (missing H on oxygen). CORRECT: Draw zigzag-$\text{OH}$ to distinguish from ethyl methyl ether (zigzag-$\text{O}$-zigzag). The H on oxygen is essential for identifying the functional group.

sections.introduction: Mastery of organic molecule drawing requires extensive practice converting between representations. This section provides progressively challenging problems to build fluency in structural, condensed, and skeletal formats.

worked_examples.title: Convert Condensed to Skeletal: 4-Ethyl-2-Methylheptane
worked_examples.problem: Draw the line-angle diagram for 4-ethyl-2-methylheptane given the condensed formula: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)\text{CH}_2\text{CH}(\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3)\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the parent chain: heptane = 7 carbons in continuous sequence.
Locate substituents from condensed formula: methyl at C2, ethyl at C4.
Draw the 7-carbon zigzag; add a short line at vertex 2 for methyl; add a 2-segment branch at vertex 4 for ethyl.
Verify: count vertices = 7 in chain + 1 in methyl + 2 in ethyl = 10 total carbons. Molecular formula: $\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{22}$.

worked_examples.final_answer: Line-angle diagram: 7-carbon zigzag with methyl branch at second vertex and ethyl branch (2-segment line) at fourth vertex. All hydrogens implied.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Condensed → skeletal: map carbons to vertices, branches to additional lines, omit C-bonded H's.

image.caption: 4-Ethyl-2-methylheptane skeletal: 7-carbon chain with methyl at C2, ethyl at C4.

worked_examples.title: Convert Skeletal to Condensed: Cyclic Alcohol
worked_examples.problem: Given a skeletal diagram of cyclohexanol (hexagon with $-\text{OH}$ at one vertex), write the condensed structural formula.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the ring: cyclohexane = 6 carbons in a closed loop.
Locate the functional group: $-\text{OH}$ attached to one ring carbon.
Write condensed formula: start at the $-\text{OH}$ carbon, write $\text{CH}(\text{OH})$, then continue around the ring with $\text{CH}_2$ groups.
Verify molecular formula: 6 carbons, 11 hydrogens on carbons + 1 hydrogen on oxygen = $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}$.

worked_examples.final_answer: Condensed formula: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{11}\text{OH}$ or $\text{CH}(\text{OH})(\text{CH}_2)_5$. Cyclohexanol: cyclic alcohol with $-\text{OH}$ on one ring carbon.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Skeletal → condensed: count ring carbons, identify substituents, write linear formula with parentheses for branches/rings.

image.alt: Cyclohexanol shown in skeletal and condensed formats
image.caption: Cyclohexanol: skeletal (hexagon with $-\text{OH}$) ↔ condensed ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{11}\text{OH}$).

worked_examples.title: Draw All Representations: 3-Hexyne
worked_examples.problem: Draw the structural formula, condensed formula, and line-angle diagram for 3-hexyne.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the molecule: 6-carbon chain with triple bond between carbons 3 and 4.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Parent: hexane} \quad \text{Functional group: triple bond at C3-C4}$

step_by_step.explanation: Draw structural formula: $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$ with all bonds shown; triple bond as three parallel lines.
Write condensed formula: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{C}\equiv\text{CCH}_2\text{CH}_3$ or $\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)\text{C}\equiv\text{C}(\text{CH}_2)\text{CH}_3$.
Draw line-angle diagram: zigzag line with triple bond (three parallel lines) between vertices 3 and 4; all hydrogens implied.

worked_examples.final_answer: 3-Hexyne representations: Structural: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{C}\equiv\text{CCH}_2\text{CH}_3$ with all bonds drawn. Condensed: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{C}\equiv\text{CCH}_2\text{CH}_3$. Skeletal: zigzag with triple bond at center. Molecular formula: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Alkyne drawing: triple bond shown as three lines in structural/skeletal; linear geometry ($180^\circ$) at $sp$ carbons.

image.alt: 3-Hexyne shown in structural, condensed, and skeletal formats
image.caption: 3-Hexyne in three representations: structural (detailed), condensed (compact), skeletal (efficient).

sections.introduction: As organic molecules become more complex, representing stereochemistry (3D arrangement) becomes essential. This section introduces conventions for depicting tetrahedral stereocenters, double bond geometry, and ring conformations in 2D drawings.

categories.description: Solid wedges indicate bonds coming out of the plane toward the viewer; dashed wedges indicate bonds going behind the plane; normal lines are in the plane
categories.detailed_mechanism: When a carbon has four different substituents (stereocenter), use wedge-dash notation to specify absolute configuration. The solid wedge ($\blacktriangle$) shows a bond projecting forward; the dashed wedge ($\dashv$) shows a bond projecting backward; two bonds in the plane are drawn as normal lines.

examples.explanation: Draw the chiral carbon with four bonds: $\text{Cl}$ on solid wedge (forward), $\text{H}$ on dashed wedge (back), $\text{CH}_3$ and $\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$ in plane. This specifies the R configuration.

image.caption: Wedge-dash: solid = forward, dashed = back, normal = in plane.

categories.description: Draw double bonds with substituents on same side (cis) or opposite sides (trans) to indicate geometric isomerism
categories.detailed_mechanism: For alkenes with two different substituents on each double-bonded carbon, draw the double bond with $120^\circ$ angles and place substituents to show relative orientation. Cis: bulky groups on same side; Trans: bulky groups on opposite sides.

examples.explanation: cis: both methyl groups drawn on same side of double bond. trans: methyl groups on opposite sides. The drawing must clearly show the relative positions to avoid ambiguity.

categories.description: Draw cyclohexane rings in chair conformation to show axial/equatorial positions and stereochemistry
categories.detailed_mechanism: The chair conformation shows cyclohexane with minimal strain. Axial bonds are drawn vertically (up/down); equatorial bonds are drawn at angles roughly in the ring plane. Substituents in axial vs equatorial positions have different steric environments.

examples.explanation: Draw the chair skeleton; place the methyl group either axial (vertical bond) or equatorial (angled bond). The equatorial conformer is more stable due to reduced 1,3-diaxial interactions.

image.caption: Chair conformation: axial (vertical) vs equatorial (angled) substituent positions.

sections.section_title: Summary Checklist for Drawing Organic Molecules
sections.introduction: A quick-reference checklist consolidates best practices for drawing organic molecules in all three representations. Use this guide to verify accuracy and clarity in your structural drawings.

components.composition: Show all atoms and bonds explicitly
components.function: Clarify bonding patterns and electron distribution
components.key_feature: Verify tetravalency: C=4 bonds, H=1, O=2 (+2 lone pairs), N=3 (+1 lone pair)

components.composition: Write carbons in sequence with attached H's; use parentheses for branches
components.key_feature: Subscripts for repeats: $\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_n\text{CH}_3$; functional groups shown explicitly

components.composition: Vertices = carbons; lines = bonds; omit C-bonded H's; show heteroatoms
components.key_feature: H count per C = 4 minus shown bonds; heteroatom H's shown explicitly
Verify molecular formula and connectivity at each conversion step

components.composition: Wedge-dash for chiral centers; cis/trans for alkenes; chair for cyclohexane
components.key_feature: Solid wedge = forward; dashed wedge = back; normal line = in plane

components.composition: Check hydrogen counts, branch placement, multiple bond geometry, heteroatom H's
components.key_feature: Common mistakes: wrong H count, ambiguous branches, incorrect alkene geometry, missing heteroatom H's

summary: Organic molecules can be represented in three primary formats: structural formulas (all atoms and bonds explicit), condensed formulas (linear text with grouped atoms), and line-angle diagrams (skeletal structures with implied hydrogens). Structural formulas clarify bonding for learning but become cluttered for large molecules. Condensed formulas save space while preserving connectivity, using parentheses for branches and subscripts for repeats. Line-angle diagrams are most efficient: vertices represent carbons, lines represent bonds, hydrogens on carbons are implied (count = 4 minus shown bonds), and heteroatoms with attached hydrogens are shown explicitly. Proficiency requires bidirectional conversion between all three formats. Best practices: verify tetravalency for each atom, use appropriate bond angles to convey hybridization ($109.5^\circ$ for $sp^3$, $120^\circ$ for $sp^2$, $180^\circ$ for $sp$), draw branches clearly originating from vertices, and show heteroatom hydrogens explicitly. Common mistakes include incorrect hydrogen counts, ambiguous branch placement, misrepresenting multiple bond geometry, and omitting hydrogens on heteroatoms. Advanced topics include wedge-dash notation for tetrahedral stereocenters (solid wedge = forward, dashed = back), cis-trans notation for alkene geometry, and chair conformations for cyclohexane stereochemistry. Mastery requires extensive practice converting between representations while verifying molecular formulas and connectivity at each step.
